name: Procedural Quests
description: A system that assembles quest objectives, targets, locations, rewards, and text from templates and world state.
design_notes: Procedural quests need more validation than random objective lists. They should track quest_template_id, objective_node_id, target_id, location constraints, reward rules, narrative tags, and soft-lock checks so generated tasks are possible and coherent.
design_purpose: Create repeatable goals for large worlds, live systems, or sandbox play.
Reuse quest structures while adapting targets, locations, rewards, or factions to current state.
Give players direction without hand-authoring every possible task.
player_fantasy: The player feels the world can produce new errands, contracts, rumors, or opportunities that fit their current journey.

parameters.name: quest_template_id
parameters.description: Template that defines objective slots, validation rules, and reward expectations.

parameters.name: objective_node_id
parameters.description: Stable identifier for each generated objective step.

parameters.name: target_id
parameters.description: Actor, item, group, or place assigned to an objective.

parameters.name: placement_rule_id
parameters.description: Rule used to choose valid quest locations and targets.

parameters.name: validation_retries
parameters.description: Attempts to assemble a valid quest before using fallback or no quest.

edge_cases: Generated target_id is killed, looted, moved, or despawned before the quest is accepted or completed.
Objective order sends players through locked areas, missing traversal abilities, or invalid biomes.
Generated text references a faction, item, or location that validation later replaces.
Rewards are invalid for the player's level or economy state after quest generation but before turn-in.
common_bugs: Quest generator validates location reachability but not whether the required item or NPC can spawn there.
Template variables are replaced in UI text but not in tracking markers or save data.
validation_retries eventually accept a quest with missing objective_node_id because fallback checks are weaker.
Generated quests duplicate the same target_id and conflict with each other.
balancing_notes: Procedural quests need caps and cooldowns so generated chores do not overwhelm authored goals.
Reward value should account for travel distance, risk, required items, and expected time.
A few strong templates with good validation are usually better than many shallow objective combinations.
accessibility_notes: Quest text should clearly name target, location, reward, and next step; avoid vague generated phrasing.
Markers, logs, and map filters should support players who cannot memorize generated names or routes.

implementation_notes.unity: Build quests from quest_template_id data, validate objective_node_id reachability and target existence, then persist the generated quest payload before showing UI or markers.
implementation_notes.godot: Use Resource or JSON quest templates with explicit slots; run world-state validation and text binding after target selection, then save stable target IDs and objective IDs.
implementation_notes.unreal: Use DataAssets for templates and a quest subsystem for generation/validation; server or authoritative save logic should own generated quest IDs in shared worlds.
implementation_notes.web: Represent generated quests as serialized data with template ID, filled slots, content_version, and validation trace so bug reports can reproduce bad quest rolls.

example_games.title: The Elder Scrolls V: Skyrim
example_games.note: Uses radiant-style quests to create repeatable objectives.

example_games.title: No Man's Sky
example_games.note: Uses generated missions and objectives in a procedural universe.
